our_argument_summary: Our review claims the hemispheric magnetic dipole decay asymmetry ratio is a consequence of reversed-flux patches in the outer core, modeled by CHAOS-7 using spherical harmonic expansions. The dome model has no core dynamics, no MHD equations, and cannot derive the ratio from its geometry. It observes the ratio and claims it; CHAOS-7 derives it from first principles. Verdict: Std Model Explains.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt — WIN-005 references CHAOS-7 and 'asymmetric decay predicted by aetheric field geometry'
raw-text/01-home.txt — CHAOS-7 paleomagnetic records cited as data source
monitor.py — not available in raw-text directory; no WIN-063-specific monitoring domain identified from prior reviews of group members (WIN-040, 041, 042, 043, 059, 060, 061)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline identified for WIN-063. The dome's source material (raw-text/02-wins.txt) states 'Asymmetric decay predicted by aetheric field geometry' at WIN-005, with source 'CHAOS-7. African cell: 23,050 to 21,880 nT. American cell decays slower.' WIN-063 appears to re-slice this same asymmetry observation as a 'ratio' metric. No formula for deriving the asymmetry ratio from dome geometry (H(r), disc_radius, or aetheric vortex parameters) is provided. The claim is asserted, not computed.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring domain computes or validates the asymmetry ratio. Based on group member reviews (WIN-040 through WIN-061), the geomagnetic monitoring domains in monitor.py use hardcoded thresholds or have no validation at all. WIN-063 has no known dedicated domain.
data_sources_used: CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model (DTU Space, Copenhagen)
NOAA data (generic citation, same as WIN-004/005)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome does not specify which 'ratio' it predicts, what time period is used, or how 'hemispheric' is defined on a flat disc geometry. Without specifying the predicted ratio value, any observed asymmetry can be claimed as 'matching' after the fact. The word 'matches' in the claim implies quantitative agreement but no numerical prediction is stated.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not make code-specific claims about WIN-063's monitoring. The review correctly states the dome 'observes the ratio and claims it' which is consistent with the absence of any monitoring domain for this WIN.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitoring domain identified for WIN-063 in the dome's monitoring infrastructure. Prior curmudgeon reviews of all other group members (WIN-040 through WIN-061) mapped the geomagnetic monitoring domains. None computes a hemispheric decay asymmetry ratio. WIN-005 (which shares the asymmetry theme) uses a hardcoded field strength threshold, not a ratio computation. WIN-063's specific claim — a 'ratio' metric — has no automated validation whatsoever.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Hemispheric asymmetry in geomagnetic field decay is well-established in geomagnetism. The CHAOS-7 model (Finlay et al., 2020, Earth Planets Space) describes outer core dynamics using time-dependent spherical harmonic expansions up to degree/order 20. Reversed-flux patches beneath South Africa and South America produce asymmetric secular variation. Gubbins (1987, Nature) and Terra-Nova et al. (2017, PNAS) explain the physical mechanism: core-mantle boundary heat flux heterogeneity drives reversed-flux patch formation, which preferentially weakens the southern hemisphere dipole. The dome model renames this as 'aetheric field geometry' without changing the observed value or providing an alternative derivation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Hemispheric asymmetry in field decay has been documented since at least Gubbins (1987). CHAOS-7 and its predecessors (CHAOS-1 through CHAOS-6) have quantified this asymmetry since 2006. The dome model's WIN-063 is new in V51.0 (2026), adopting a phenomenon documented for nearly 40 years as a 'confirmed prediction.' The dome's own earlier WIN-005 already cited the same African/American cell decay asymmetry from CHAOS-7 data, making WIN-063 a re-slicing of WIN-005's data into a 'ratio' format.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome model's H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km equation is axially symmetric — it depends only on radial distance from the disc center. An axially symmetric geometry cannot derive hemispheric asymmetry without breaking its own symmetry. No formula on the dome site derives a hemispheric decay ratio from dome parameters. The dome's 'aetheric field geometry' assertion is qualitative, not quantitative. To derive an asymmetry ratio, the model would need: (1) a mechanism for creating two distinct regions with different decay rates, (2) equations that predict the ratio's specific value. Neither exists. The ratio is adopted from CHAOS-7 observations.

holes_found.description: Our review claims 'CHAOS-7 derives it from first principles' but this overstates CHAOS-7's role. CHAOS-7 is a spherical harmonic fit to satellite and observatory data — it is a data product, not a first-principles derivation. The first-principles explanation comes from MHD core dynamics (geodynamo simulations like Aubert et al., 2013). CHAOS-7 describes the field; geodynamo theory explains it. A dome defender familiar with geomagnetism could point out this conflation: 'CHAOS-7 is just a data fit too — your review criticizes us for fitting to data while praising a model that also fits to data.'
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'CHAOS-7 derives it from first principles' to: 'CHAOS-7 describes the field evolution using spherical harmonic decomposition of satellite/observatory data, while MHD geodynamo simulations (e.g., Aubert et al. 2013, Nature) derive hemispheric asymmetry from first principles via core-mantle boundary heat flux heterogeneity.' This separates the descriptive model (CHAOS-7) from the explanatory theory (core dynamics).

holes_found.description: WIN-063 is extremely vague — it claims a 'hemispheric magnetic dipole decay asymmetry ratio matches dome model' without specifying what the ratio IS, what numerical value the dome predicts, or what dataset confirms it. Our review doesn't call out this fundamental vagueness forcefully enough. The detail_claim says 'ratio matches dome model' but our rebuttal focuses on mechanism rather than the fact that no specific ratio is stated or predicted.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence: 'The dome model states the asymmetry ratio "matches" without specifying: (a) what numerical ratio is claimed, (b) over what time period, (c) using what definition of "hemispheric" on a flat disc, or (d) what the dome model actually predicts the ratio to be. Without a specific predicted value, the claim is unfalsifiable — any observed asymmetry could be retroactively declared to "match."'

holes_found.description: Our review says the dome 'has no core dynamics, no MHD equations.' While literally true, a dome defender could counter: 'We don't need a core — the asymmetry arises from our aetheric vortex topology, which has different geometry in the two hemispheres.' The dome's model does have radial asymmetry in the firmament height function H(r) and potentially in the toroidal aetheric flow. We don't address whether any of these dome-specific features could, even qualitatively, produce an asymmetry.
holes_found.recommendation: Add: 'Although the dome model's H(r) function is axially symmetric (depending only on radius), the dome does describe toroidal aetheric flow with potential directional structure. However, no published formula connects this flow topology to a specific hemispheric decay asymmetry ratio. The dome's claim requires showing how its geometry produces different decay rates on opposite sides of the disc, which has not been attempted.'

holes_found.description: The CHAOS-7 citation URL (https://spacecenter.dk/files/magnetic-models/CHAOS-7/) is a DTU Space file repository, not a peer-reviewed paper citation. While it's the legitimate CHAOS-7 data hosting site, a more authoritative citation would be the Finlay et al. (2020) paper: doi:10.1186/s40623-020-01252-9.
holes_found.recommendation: Add the formal paper citation: 'Finlay, C.C. et al. (2020), The CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model, Earth Planets Space, 72, 156, doi:10.1186/s40623-020-01252-9' alongside or replacing the current data repository URL.

holes_found.description: WIN-063 is in the group 'WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063' and our DOCX build (build-doc-v4.js) groups these as 'Geomagnetic Re-slicing' with a shared paragraph. The detail text in wins.json for WIN-063 is individual and self-contained, but the generated DOCX uses a combined group writeup. These two representations could diverge if one is edited without the other.
holes_found.recommendation: No text change needed, but flag for the integrity agent: verify that the grouped DOCX text and the individual HTML detail for WIN-063 convey the same argument. Currently they do, but future edits to either could create inconsistency.

stronger_arguments.description: WIN-063 is a transparent re-slicing of WIN-005's data. WIN-005 already claims 'African SAA cell decays faster' citing CHAOS-7 asymmetric decay. WIN-063 takes the same observation and reframes it as a 'ratio.' This is not a new prediction — it is arithmetic on an already-counted WIN. The review should explicitly note: 'WIN-063 extracts a ratio from the same CHAOS-7 data already cited in WIN-005. Computing a ratio from one WIN's data does not create an independent second WIN.'

Axial symmetry contradiction: The dome's H(r) function depends only on radial distance r and is perfectly axially symmetric. A perfectly symmetric geometry cannot produce hemispheric asymmetry. This is a stronger argument than 'no core dynamics' because it shows the dome's own geometry actively contradicts the claim. The dome would need to break its own symmetry to derive a hemispheric ratio, which would require additional free parameters not acknowledged in their 'zero fitted parameters' claim.

The dome's claim says 'matches dome model' without specifying what the dome model predicts. This is a pattern seen across V51.0 WINs: assert a match without stating the predicted value. For WIN-063, the specific question should be: 'What numerical ratio does the dome model predict, and from what equation was it derived?' If no ratio is stated, the claim is unfalsifiable.
sources: Pattern documented across WIN-040-043 group reviews

kernel_of_truth.description: Hemispheric asymmetry in geomagnetic field decay is real and scientifically interesting
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The asymmetry is genuine — the South Atlantic Anomaly region and reversed-flux patches beneath southern Africa produce measurable differences in dipole moment decay between hemispheres. This is well-documented by CHAOS-7 and Swarm satellite data. However, observing a real phenomenon does not constitute prediction. The dome model provides no formula, no geometric derivation, and no specific numerical ratio derived from its equations. Any model that claims to describe the geomagnetic field must account for this asymmetry; doing so is necessary but not sufficient. Standard geomagnetism explains the asymmetry via core-mantle boundary heat flux heterogeneity (higher heat flux under the Pacific) driving reversed-flux patch formation — a testable, falsifiable mechanism. The dome's 'aetheric field geometry' explanation provides no comparable testable mechanism.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-063 exemplifies three of the dome model's most persistent patterns simultaneously: (1) re-slicing — it takes the same CHAOS-7 asymmetric decay data already cited in WIN-005 and repackages it as a 'ratio' to create a new WIN; (2) vagueness-as-unfalsifiability — by saying the ratio 'matches' without specifying what ratio was predicted, any observed value confirms the claim; (3) relabeling — the asymmetry mechanism (reversed-flux patches in the outer core) is renamed as 'aetheric field geometry' without changing the predicted value or providing an alternative derivation.

The strongest technical objection is the axial symmetry point: the dome's own H(r) equation is purely radial. You cannot derive a hemispheric (i.e., angular) asymmetry from a function that has no angular dependence. This is a mathematical impossibility, not a physical argument — it follows directly from the dome's own published equations. To derive a hemispheric ratio, the dome would need to introduce angular-dependent parameters, which would increase the model's free parameter count and undermine the 'zero fitted parameters' claim made elsewhere (SEC-3.5.1).

The CHAOS-7 citation is adequate but could be strengthened with the formal paper reference (Finlay et al. 2020). Our claim that 'CHAOS-7 derives from first principles' is imprecise — CHAOS-7 is a data product. The first-principles derivation comes from geodynamo theory (core-mantle coupling). This distinction matters because a knowledgeable dome defender could exploit the conflation.

Overall, our verdict of 'Std Model Explains' is correct and well-supported. The main improvement needed is acknowledging the CHAOS-7 ≠ first-principles distinction and calling out the axial symmetry contradiction more forcefully.

citations_verified: CHAOS-7 (https://spacecenter.dk/files/magnetic-models/CHAOS-7/) — Valid DTU Space data repository for the CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model. Site resolves correctly and hosts model coefficients.
NOAA (https://www.noaa.gov/) — Generic homepage; resolves but is not a specific dataset citation
citations_failed: NOAA (https://www.noaa.gov/) — Same issue flagged in WIN-004, 005, 060 reviews: this is a homepage, not a data source. Should cite specific NOAA product: World Magnetic Model (WMM2025), IGRF-14, or NCEI geomagnetic data portal (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/world-magnetic-model)
citations_unchecked: Whether CHAOS-7 specifically quantifies a 'hemispheric decay asymmetry ratio' — it describes secular variation in spherical harmonics, but the specific ratio metric may need a derived calculation not directly available from CHAOS-7 output files
Whether Finlay et al. (2020, doi:10.1186/s40623-020-01252-9) is the correct CHAOS-7 reference or if a newer version has been published
